name: La Tomatina
tagline: For one delirious hour, a quiet Valencian hill town drowns itself in a hundred tonnes of overripe tomatoes.
dates: Wed 26 Aug 2026 (confirmed); Wed 25 Aug 2027 (expected) — always the last Wednesday of August, fight from 12:00 to 13:00
history: La Tomatina was never planned. In August 1945, during Buñol's giants-and-big-heads parade, a scuffle broke out in the Plaza del Pueblo — one of the costumed 'big-head' figures toppled over, tempers flared, and the crowd grabbed the nearest ammunition: tomatoes from a market stall. The brawl was so gloriously fun that the town's young people came back the next year with their own tomatoes and did it on purpose. A tradition was born from an accident, which remains very much the spirit of the thing.

Franco's regime banned the festival in the early 1950s for having no religious significance, and locals were arrested for throwing tomatoes anyway. Buñol's answer, in 1957, was pure theatre: a mock 'tomato funeral,' with residents solemnly parading a coffin containing a giant tomato through town. The protest worked, the ban was lifted, and Spanish television coverage in the following decades turned a village lark into a global phenomenon. In 2002 Spain's tourism ministry declared it a Fiesta of International Tourist Interest.

By the 2010s the crush of visitors — up to 50,000 in a town of 9,000 — forced organisers to introduce paid tickets in 2013, capping the fight at roughly 20,000 participants. Around 100–145 tonnes of over-ripe, purpose-grown tomatoes are trucked in each year and hurled for exactly one hour, bracketed by starter-pistol shots. Before the tomatoes fly, daredevils shinny up the 'palo jabón,' a soap-greased pole topped with a Spanish ham. Afterwards, fire crews hose down the streets — the tomatoes' citric acid leaves the cobbles cleaner than before — and locals hose down the participants. The festival has inspired copycat tomato fights from Colorado to China.
whyGo: It is the world's biggest food fight, and no photo prepares you for standing waist-deep in a river of tomato pulp while an entire town cheers. The event is one perfectly contained hour of pure, absurd catharsis — bookended by a Spanish village fiesta with brass bands, paella and hose-downs from grandmothers on balconies. From Zürich it's an easy long-weekend hit: two hours to Valencia, one commuter train to Buñol.

gettingThere.route: Zürich (ZRH) → Valencia (VLC) direct with Vueling or SWISS/Edelweiss, then Cercanías C-3 train or festival shuttle bus Valencia → Buñol
gettingThere.notes: Book the flight 2–3 months out; late-August fares spike. On fight day take the earliest C-3 from Valencia Sant Isidre (~6–8am) or a package shuttle — trains are rammed and Buñol station is a walk from the plaza.

gettingThere.route: Zürich HB → Geneva (IC) → Barcelona Sants (TGV Lyria/SNCF) → Valencia Joaquín Sorolla (AVE/Euromed), overnight in Barcelona or Valencia
gettingThere.notes: Scenic, flight-free option. Book SNCF Zürich→Barcelona ~3 months out for the cheap tiers; Barcelona→Valencia AVE takes ~2h40.

gettingThere.route: Zürich → Lyon → Montpellier → Barcelona → Valencia → Buñol via A9/AP-7/A-3
gettingThere.notes: Only worth it as part of a longer Spain road trip. On the day itself, park in Valencia and take the train — Buñol closes to traffic and parking is hopeless.

costs.notes: The fight ticket itself is cheap (roughly €15–25 basic entry); the money goes on the late-August flight from Zürich and 2–3 nights in Valencia, where festival week pushes hotel rates up. Splurge packages (€400–700) bundle guaranteed entry, shuttle, breakfast, lockers and post-fight showers; thrifty travellers book a hostel in Valencia and the €8 commuter train.

booking.label: Official ticket portal — Tomatina de Buñol
Official festival info — La Tomatina (Buñol town hall)

weather: Late August in inland Valencia is hot and dry — 30–33°C at midday, strong sun, near-zero rain. Pack throwaway clothes and shoes you'll bin afterwards, swim goggles, a waterproof phone pouch, sunscreen, and a full change of clothes in a sealed bag left at a locker or with your tour.
tips: Buy your official ticket the moment sales open (usually spring) — the 20,000 cap sells out, and only ticketed wristbands get past the barriers.
Sleep in Valencia, not Buñol. The town has almost no beds; the C-3 commuter train and festival shuttles do the 38 km in under an hour. Be in Buñol by 8–9am to get into the plaza.

Wear clothes and closed shoes you will throw away, plus swim goggles — tomato acid stings, and flip-flops get sucked off your feet in the pulp. Leave cameras behind or bring a strapped waterproof pouch.
Know the rules: squash every tomato before throwing, don't hurl bottles or tear T-shirts, keep clear of the tomato trucks, and stop dead at the second pistol shot at 13:00.
Afterwards, follow the locals: villagers hose down strangers from garden hoses, and many participants rinse off at the Cueva Turche waterfall pools just outside town.
Skip the fight-day paella scrum and stay for the week if you can — Tomatina caps a full local fiesta with parades, music and the palo jabón ham pole on the morning itself.

localRecs.name: Castillo de Buñol
localRecs.note: A 13th-century Moorish-founded castle rising straight out of the old town — climb it the evening before the fight for views over the plaza that will be tomato-red by noon.

localRecs.name: Cueva Turche
localRecs.note: A 60-metre waterfall and natural pool 10 minutes from Buñol — the classic post-Tomatina rinse and picnic spot.

localRecs.name: Venta L'Home
localRecs.note: Historic roadside inn just outside Buñol on the A-3, serving proper Valencian arroces and game dishes — book for the night before the fight.

localRecs.name: Mercado Central & Barrio del Carmen, Valencia
localRecs.note: Base yourself here: Europe's most beautiful market hall for breakfast, medieval lanes and tapas bars for the post-fight celebration night.

localRecs.name: Horchatería Santa Catalina, Valencia
localRecs.note: Century-old institution for ice-cold horchata and fartons — exactly what a body pickled in tomato and sun wants at 5pm.

photos.caption: The crowd mid-fight in Buñol's packed streets, ankle-deep in tomato pulp (2010)
A tomato truck ploughs through the crowd on Buñol's main street
